coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots embedded bacterial cellulose membrane as active packaging: Toxicity, in vitro release and application in minced beef packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Ghorbani, Mahdi
Moradi, Mehran
Tajik, Hossein
Molaei, Rahim
Alizadeh, Arash
coredata.dc:description: Antimicrobial bacterial cellulose (BC) membranes incorporated with carbon dots (CDs) were developed to improve the shelf life and ensure the safety of minced beef during 9 days of storage at 4 °C. An ex-situ method was used to develop BC-CDs with different CDs loading capacities (16.50, 22.50, and 38.50 mg/cm3). Only BC-CDs38.50 membrane exhibited toxicity in human embryonic kidney cells, and BC-CDs membranes had the slowest release rate of CDs in 95% ethanol. Significant differences were noted in the chemical and sensory attributes of samples packaged with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50, compared to the control. The microbial counts in samples with BC-CDs were significantly lower than those in samples with pristine BC membranes or the control. Notably, the BC-CDs22.50 membrane exhibited a substantial reduction (4.7 log10 CFU/g) in Escherichia coli counts by the end of storage. These findings highlight the potential of BC-CDs membranes as effective antimicrobial materials in meat packaging.

Release rate of the CDs from the BC membranes (BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50) in various food simulant solutions (10%, 50% and 95% ethanol) during 24 h exposure (B).

(c) Efficacy of bacterial cellulose (BC) and BC containing carbon dots (BC-CDs) with a loading capacity of 16.50 mg/cm3 and 22.50 mg/cm3 on E. coli O157:H7 counts (Log10 CFU/g) in minced meat during refrigerator storage (values with different letters are significantly different; P ≤ 0.05).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots embedded bacterial cellulose membrane as active packaging: Toxicity, in vitro release and application in minced beef packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial bacterial cellulose (BC) membranes incorporated with carbon dots (CDs) were developed to improve the shelf life and ensure the safety of minced beef during 9 days of storage at 4 °C. An ex-situ method was used to develop BC-CDs with different CDs loading capacities (16.50, 22.50, and 38.50 mg/cm3). Only BC-CDs38.50 membrane exhibited toxicity in human embryonic kidney cells, and BC-CDs membranes had the slowest release rate of CDs in 95% ethanol. Significant differences were noted in the chemical and sensory attributes of samples packaged with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50, compared to the control. The microbial counts in samples with BC-CDs were significantly lower than those in samples with pristine BC membranes or the control. Notably, the BC-CDs22.50 membrane exhibited a substantial reduction (4.7 log10 CFU/g) in Escherichia coli counts by the end of storage. These findings highlight the potential of BC-CDs membranes as effective antimicrobial materials in meat packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat products undergo spoilage in the supply chain owing to the presence of high levels of unsaturated fatty acids, low oxidative stability, and vulnerability to bacterial and fungal growth, leading to off-flavor and discoloration. To prevent the growth of spoilage microbiota and preserve quality, various types of antimicrobials derived from plants, microorganisms, animals, and algae can be used directly or indirectly (Alirezalu et al., 2021). The meat processing industry considers active packaging, particularly antimicrobial packaging, as a novel approach to prolong shelf life and ensure the safety of meat by releasing antimicrobial substances from the designed packaging film (Ahmed et al., 2022). The sustained and controlled release of these antimicrobials from the film onto the food matrix is highly dependent on the type of film matrix (synthetic or biopolymer) and the approach used to develop film, also the migration of active agents can be controlled during the shelf life of food (Barbosa-Pereira et al., 2014).

As an alternative to non-degradable polymers and to address environmental problems of using plastics, microbial biopolymers, particularly bacterial cellulose (BC), have gained considerable interest in antimicrobial packaging in recent years (Kumar et al., 2022). BC is synthesized by bacterial species, especially Komagataeibacter, and is recognized as an excellent biopolymer in the food packaging industry thanks to its numerous advantages such as accessible functional groups, appealing mechanical properties, a three-dimensional/high-porosity network, large specific surface area, high water-holding capacity, and high crystallinity (Kumar et al., 2022; Moradi, Jacek et al., 2021). Therefore, BC has been used as an appropriate platform for the loading of various antimicrobials (Almasi et al., 2019; Rasouli et al., 2021; Shafipour Yordshahi et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2022).

The application of carbon dots (CDs) in active food packaging has gained momentum owing to their remarkable zero-dimensional, fluorescent properties, high water solubility, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, UV protection, antioxidant features, and antimicrobial characteristics (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022; Moradi, Molaei, et al., 2021). CDs are commonly synthesized using a bottom-up approach, which involves an inexpensive and straightforward method using chemicals, natural materials, food waste, and microorganisms (Moradi, Molaei, et al., 2021). This synthesis process employs various techniques such as ultrasonic-assisted, hydrothermal/solvothermal, and microwave-assisted methods (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, et al., 2022). The ability to manipulate CDs properties during synthesis has made these nanomaterials particularly attractive for antimicrobial applications (Lacivita et al., 2023). The antimicrobial activity of CDs is associated to their size, shape, surface charge, and functional groups and involves multiple mechanisms, including reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, cell structure breakdown, cytoplasmic leakage, and degradation of proteins and DNA (Dong et al., 2020; Esmaeili Koutamehr et al., 2023). Different research groups have explored the antimicrobial efficacy of different films and coating materials containing CDs including alginate coating on bacterial and fungal population of mozzarella cheese (Lacivita et al., 2023), cellulose film on fungal population of strawberry (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022), chitosan/gelatin coating on fungal population of avocado (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022), chitosan/gelatin film on bacterial population of minced beef (Sul et al., 2023), chitosan/gelatin film on bacterial population of fish meat (Fu et al., 2022), gelatin/polyvinyl alcohol film on Listeria monocytogenes in pork (Min et al., 2023) and chitosan film on bacterial population of pork (Chen et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2023).

Previously, we synthesized CDs from Saccharomyces cerevisiae postbiotics with an average size of 4.1 nm and a zeta potential of −47.6 mV (Ghorbani et al., 2022). The presence of N and P in the CDs structure was confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analysis. The CDs solution exhibited a yellow–brown color under white light and bright green color under ultraviolet light. When added to the BNC, the CDs did not alter the fundamental arrangement and porous network of the BC membrane but rather filled the space between the fibrils and irregular pores. The antimicrobial performances of fungi (Aspergillus flavus, Rhodotorula rubra, Aspergillus fumigatus, and Fusarium solani) and bacteria (L. monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella Typhimurium, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) were investigated using a well diffusion assay. Additionally, we formulated BC membranes containing CDs (BC-CDs) that displayed sufficient antibacterial activity against selected foodborne pathogens and spoilage microorganisms under in vitro tests according to the disk-covering test. The antioxidant activity, cytotoxicity, spectral specifications, and mechanical strength of BC-CDs were also investigated (Ghorbani et al., 2022). In line with earlier work, in this work, BC-CDs membranes were applied for the packaging of minced beef stored at 4 °C. The toxicity and release rate of BC membranes containing varying concentrations of CDs were initially evaluated, and the optimized membranes were then utilized in minced beef to extend their shelf life while controlling the growth of E. coli O157:H7, a significant bacterial pathogen affecting meat products.

BC membranes were produced by Komagataeibacter xylinus BPR 2001 under static conditions as described by Rodrigues et al. (2019). A hydrothermal technique was applied to synthesize CDs from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae.

BC-CDs membranes were prepared using an ex-situ method by submerging the BC membrane in a beaker containing filtered CDs (25, 50, and 100 mg/mL) at ambient temperature for 24 h to obtain loading capacities of 16.50, 22.50, and 38.50 mg/cm3, respectively. These values were determined by measuring the absorption of the CDs solution at 365 nm before and after loading into the BC membrane using a NanoDrop microvolume spectrophotometer (NanoDrop 2000c, Waltham, MA, USA). Based on the absorbance readings of the calibration curve, we quantified the amount of CDs loaded in each membrane. The amount of CDs absorbed per cm2 of dried BC was used to obtain the results. The BC-CDs membranes were dried at 40 °C for 12 h and used in subsequent experiments.

To select BC-CDs membranes with no cytotoxic activity, cytotoxicity assessment was conducted according to the method described by Nwabor et al. (2020). To this end, 1 and 5 mg of BC-CDs membranes (with a loading capacity of 16.50, 22.50, and 38.50 mg/cm3) were placed separately in test tubes containing PBS (Sigma Chemical, Saint Louis, MO, USA, pH 7) and shaken at 100 rpm for 24 h. The toxicity of the medium was assessed by the MTT test on two cell lines: human colon cancer (HCT-116) and human embryonic kidney (HEK) cell lines (Ghorbani et al., 2022).

CDs release from BC-CDs membranes was assessed using food simulants based on Razavi, et al. (2020), with modifications. To simulate aqueous foods, oil-in-water emulsion foods, and fatty foods, ethanol, and water were mixed at concentrations of 10, 50, and 95% concentrations to prepare food simulants. Tow-selected nontoxic BC-CDs membranes (with loading capacities of 16.50 and 22.50 mg/cm3) were cut into square pieces (2 × 2 cm) and added to a conical tube that already contained 20 mL of each simulant. The tubes were gently shaken (90 rpm) and kept at 25 °C for 24 h and absorbance was measured at 0, 1, 2, 4, 12, and 24-hour intervals using a NanoDrop spectrophotometer (285 nm), and the amount of released CDs was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BC-CDs membrane on minced beef
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh minced meat (pH 5.8 ± 0.1 with fat and protein contents of 4.2 ± 0.7 g and 21.6 ± 0.3 g in 100 g, respectively) was obtained from a local market in Urmia, Iran and transported under hygienic conditions at 4 ± 1 °C in the food packaging laboratory. Circular slices of selected BC membranes (9 cm in diameter) were prepared, UV-sterilized for 2 min, and immersed in two selected CDs concentrations (BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50), as described in section 2.1 . The experiment involved 50 g of minced meat with BC-CDs membranes applied to both the bottom and top surfaces of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial performance of BC-CDs membrane on E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: The E. coli O157:H7 was obtained from the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (Tehran, Iran), grown in TSB medium (Quelab, Montreal, Canada), and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h until the bacterial count reached 8 log10 CFU/mL (optical density: ∼ 0.1) via spectrophotometric assay at 600 nm, and bacterial count was confirmed by plate count method on Sorbitol MacConkey agar (Moradi et al., 2021). 0.1 mL E. coli suspension (8 log10 CFU/mL) was inoculated into 99.9 g of fresh minced meat in a sterile stomacher bag under aseptic conditions, and homogenized at 280 rpm for 3 min using a stomacher (Seward Medical Ltd. London, UK) to achieve final bacterial count of 5 log10 CFU/g. Bacterial counts were verified using the plate count method on Sorbitol MacConkey agar after serial dilutions of the sample (Shafipour Yordshahi et al., 2020). A total of 40 g of meat sample was sandwiched between two BC-CDs membranes to ensure complete coverage of the meat. The samples were divided into four groups: the control with no BC-CDs membrane, a group with BC membrane, a group with BC containing CDs with a loading capacity of 16.50 mg/cm3 (BC-CDs16.50), and a group with BC containing CDs with a loading capacity of 22.50 mg/cm3 (BC-CDs22.50). Each sample was packed by overwrapped tray method in a polyethylene box and stored at 4 ± 1 °C for 9 days. The E. coli population was counted on Sorbitol MacConkey agar (Quelab, Montreal, Canada) using the surface culture method on days 0, 3, 6, and 9. Incubation was performed at 37 ± 1 °C for 48 h.

The antimicrobial mechanism of CDs on E. coli, was assessed by SEM analysis. A bacterial suspension (106 CFU/mL) was prepared in TSB and incubated with CDs (100 µg/mL) or without CDs (control) for 24 h at 37 °C. To isolate the bacterial pellet, the solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm. The pellet was washed five times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7) and dissolved in PBS. Then, 50 μL of the bacterial suspension was spread over aluminum foil and dried. The samples were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 22 °C and dehydrated using ethanol solutions (50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 95, and 100%) for 30 min for each step. The cells were then dried at room temperature, treated with gold, and examined using SEM (JSM-6500F, JEOL Ltd., Akishima, Tokyo, Japan) (Cui et al., 2021).

The impact of BC-CDs membranes on the microbial, chemical, and sensory properties of meat was assessed at 0, 3, 6, and 9 days. To accomplish this, non-inoculated minced beef was prepared using the method explained in Section 2.4.1 , with and without BC-CDs membranes.

Microbiological analyses were performed using the standard plate counting method. At each time point, 10 g of sample was thoroughly homogenized in 90 mL of peptone water (0.1% w/v, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany). Serial dilutions of each suspension were prepared and cultured on plate count agar (Ibresco Company, Tehran, Iran). To determine the total psychrophiles count (TPC) and total mesophiles count (TMC), plates were incubated at 7 °C for 10 days and at 37 °C for 24 h.
After homogenizing 10 g of each sample in 90 mL 0.1% peptone water, a digital pH meter (Metrohm Herisau, Switzerland) was used to measure the pH of the samples.

To assess TBARS, 10 g of sample was homogenized in 97.5 mL of distilled water, and 2.5 mL of 4 M hydrochloric acid was added to adjust pH (∼1.5). Then, the homogenate was heated in a Kjeldahl flask and a mixture of 5 mL of TBARS solution (Quelab, Montreal, Canada) and 5 mL of distilled solution was prepared and heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 30 min. The absorbance of samples was measured against a blank solution (5 mL TBA and 5 mL distilled water) using a spectrophotometer (538 nm) (Pharmacia, Switzerland). The results were reported as milligrams of malondialdehyde per kilogram of meat sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N)
ce:section.ce:para:
TVB-N was determined by steam distillation (Aghaei et al., 2018). A homogenized solution of 10 g of meat sample, 300 mL of distilled water, and 2 g of MgO was transferred to a Kjeldahl flask. The resulting distillation mixture was then added to a receiving flask containing boric acid (25 mL, 2% v/v), two drops of methylene blue (0.1%), and methyl red (0.1%) as indicators. The final titration was conducted using 0.1 N sulfuric acid. The TVB-N content was reported as mg/100 g and calculated accordingly (Eq. (1)):
(1)

The volume of sulfuric acid utilized in the titration was denoted as V1.

Color parameters, including lightness (L*), redness to greenness (a*), and yellowness to blueness (b*) were measured using a Lovibond® LC 100 Spectrocolorimeter (Tintometer® Group, Lovibond house, UK). Five random points of each 10 g meat sample were assessed to determine the color. To evaluate the sensory features of the raw meat samples, such as color, odor, and overall acceptance, a structured 9-point hedonic scale was used, where 1 indicated extreme dislike, 2 indicated strong dislike, 3 indicated moderate dislike, 4 indicated slight dislike, 5 indicated no liking or disliking, 6 indicated slight liking, 7 indicated moderate liking, 8 indicated strong liking, and 9 indicated extreme liking. The samples were randomly labeled and evaluated by a panel of 21 semi-qualified members (9 males and 12 females) in individual testing booths. These booths were equipped with incandescent lighting and neutral-colored walls and were maintained at a temperature of 22 °C (Shafipour Yordshahi et al., 2020). This experiment was reviewed and approved by Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, and informed consent was obtained from each member prior to their participation in the experiment.

The meat challenge tests were conducted in a randomized design consisting of four treatments × four storage periods with two replications, and all data were reported as the mean ± SD. Three samples from each treatment group were evaluated during each storage period. The shelf-life parameters (microbiology, pH, TVB-N, and TBA) were random factors, whereas the treatments and storage time were fixed factors. In the sensory analysis, the sensory panelists were the random term and sampling times were used as fixed effects. The raw data were subjected to analysis of variance (ANOVA) using GraphPad Prism version 8.01 for Windows (San Diego, CA, USA). In addition, Tukey’s multiple-range test was used to test for significant differences at the 95% confidence level (P-value <0.05) for each variable and Principal component analysis (PCA) was performed using “factoextra” package in the R 4.2.2 program (Kassambara & Mundt, 2017).

In our previous study, we assessed the impact of CDs on the viability of HCT-116 and TM4 (mouse Sertoli cell line) cell lines. The results indicated that CDs demonstrated no cytotoxic effects on either cell line at concentrations up to 1.75 mg/L, indicating the non-toxic nature of CDs (Ghorbani et al., 2022). To utilize antimicrobial films in food packaging, the toxicity of BC-CDs membranes is of major importance for risk assessment. The extent of cytotoxicity of antimicrobial films containing nanomaterials depends on the release of nanoparticles into the food (Nwabor et al., 2020). Therefore, the prepared BC-CDs membranes were left in PBS and allowed to release CDs into the medium, and the cytotoxicity of the resulting solution was assessed. Similarly, the unmodified BC film was subjected to cytotoxicity assessment. Two different weights (1 and 5 mg) of BC and BC-CDs membranes were evaluated for their toxicity via the MTT assay in two cell lines (HEK and HCT-116) after a 24-hour exposure period (Fig. 1 A). Cell viability in the presence of 1 mg and 5 mg BC membranes was comparable to that of the control group. The results showed that BC-CDs16.50 (1 and 5 mg) caused greater cell mortality in HTC116 cells than in HEK cells. However, a significant decrease in cell viability was observed in the BC-CDs38.50 (5 mg) group for both cell lines. These findings indicated higher biocompatibility of BC and BC-CDs membranes (excluding BC-CDs38.50), as they did not show significant cytotoxicity. Cell viability above 80% can be considered a safe threshold for applications related to food packaging and biological purposes (Min et al., 2023). Considering the above-mentioned results, BC- CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 membranes were selected for subsequent trials and applications in food packaging. Several cytotoxicity studies have reported similar results for films containing nanomaterials. Alaş et al. (2022) investigated a composite polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film with multicolor (blue, green, and yellow) CDs on a healthy human lung fibroblast cell line (MRC-F cells) by MTT assay. The results showed that after 24 and 48-hour exposure, the CD/PVA films were non-cytotoxic at different concentrations of CDs (25, 50, 100, and 250 mg/mL). Priyadarshi et al. (2021) evaluated the toxicity of gelatin/agar blend films containing sulfur CDs. After extended exposure for 72 h, the films displayed negligible toxicity and extreme biocompatibility when exposed to L929 mouse fibroblasts. The cytotoxicity of CDs prepared from chitosan by the hydrothermal method was evaluated on human skin fibroblasts using the MTT assay (Fu et al., 2022). In 0.5–5 mg/L concentration of CDs, no cytotoxic effect was observed. The cytotoxicity of the synthesized CDs from spent coffee grounds at a concentration of 0.1 to 1 mg/mL was investigated on the L929 mouse fibroblast line (Min et al., 2023). No toxicity was observed at any of the tested concentrations.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the release of CDs from BC membranes, which was influenced by the type of simulant solution used in the study. The release rate of CDs varied depending on the solubility of CDs in the simulant solution, with water resulting in the fastest release and 95% ethanol resulting in the slowest release (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022; Roy et al., 2021). The rate at which the CDs were released also varied depending on their concentration, with higher concentrations resulting in higher release rates. As the alcohol concentration in the simulant solution was increased, the CDs release rate decreased. CDs were released quickly from BC-CDs22.50 and reached a steady state after 4 h of immersion in 50% ethanol. In 95% ethanol, the release rate was minimal during the first 4 h and gradually increased over time. To date, few studies have focused on incorporating CDs into food simulation models (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, et al., 2022). One study explored the release rate of enoki mushroom CDs from gelatin/carrageenan films (Roy et al., 2021). The release rates in ethanol solutions (95% and 50%) were significantly lower than those in 10% ethanol solution and water. In a separate study, the release behavior of CDs synthesized from glucose and nitrogen-doped CDs from cellulose nanofiber films in food simulant solutions was investigated (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022). These findings indicated that glucose CDs were immobilized in the cellulose matrix, reducing their mobility and diffusion in the cellulose nanofiber structures. Consequently, more glucose-derived CDs were released in the aqueous solutions than in the alcoholic solutions. Additionally, the hydrophilicity of the glucose CDs played a critical role in the release rate. In contrast, the release of nitrogen-doped CDs was greater than that of glucose CDs, likely due to the presence of nitrogen compounds such as amines and amides in nitrogen-doped CDs (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022).

The release rates of nanoparticles from BC membranes are influenced by several factors such as the degree of intermolecular interactions, pore size, moisture absorption potency, and intensity of nanoparticle entanglement in the BC network. Other factors, such as the hydrophilicity of BC and the affinity between nanoparticles and the solvent in simulant solutions, also play a role (Ezati et al., 2022). The hydrophilic nature of BC membranes allows easy penetration of aqueous solutions. When BC membranes are hydrated to form an open structure, CDs are quickly released (Roy et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BC-CDs membrane in ground beef
Antibacterial performance of BC-CDs membrane on E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: Bactericidal and bacteriostatic activities are mediated through various pathways, such as physical and mechanical damage to cell membranes, destruction of cell walls leading to cytoplasmic leakage, inactivation through localized temperature elevation via photothermal activity, direct or light-induced generation of ROS, and protein and DNA damage (Ghirardello et al., 2021). Fig. 2 c shows the effectiveness of the BC membrane on E. coli growth in minced meat with and without CDs. During nine days, samples packaged with BC did not show any anti- E. coli activity, which was similar to that of the control samples. However, samples with BC-CDs showed a significant (P < 0.05) reduction in the number of E. coli. The antibacterial activity of the BC-CDs membrane was attributed to the amount of CDs, and their diffusion from the BC membrane into the meat started immediately and continued until day nine. On day 3, samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 exhibited 0.5 and 1 log10 CFU/g reduction in E. coli, respectively. Moreover, BC-CDs22.50 was the most efficient in preventing the growth of E. coli, reducing its population from 5.2 ± 0.1 at day 0 to 0.5 ± 0.7 log10 CFU/g by day 9, compared to the control samples, which exhibited a population increase to 5.8 log10 CFU/g after 9 days of storage. This study found that CDs incorporated into BC at a non-toxic loading concentration had a positive antimicrobial effect against E. coli in meat. The inhibitory effect increased with increasing CDs content, and this difference was significant (P < 0.05) compared with the BC or control samples on all days.

The incorporation of turmeric-derived CDs and sulfur-doped CDs into pectin/gelatin films resulted in antibacterial properties, reducing the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli by approximately 2 log10 CFU/mL, while pure pectin/gelatin films exhibited no antibacterial activity (Ezati, Roy, et al., 2022). The addition of sulfur-doped CDs to the films further enhanced the antimicrobial effects owing to the presence of functional groups, such as sulfonates and sulfonic acids, that aid in enzyme binding, ROS generation, and protein or lipid degradation. In a similar study, Tariq et al. (2022) investigated the antibacterial efficacy of fig-derived CDs against E. coli and found that fig-derived CDs demonstrated greater bactericidal activity than extracted figs products. SEM analysis and nitro blue tetrazolium assay findings support the involvement of ROS generation in bacterial cell death.

Fig. 2 (a,b) presents the morphological changes in E. coli before and after treatment with the CDs obtained using SEM. The untreated cells exhibited a rod-shaped appearance with smooth and intact cell walls and rounded ends (Fig. 2a). In contrast, cells exposed to CDs showed distorted structures with wrinkled surfaces, volume reduction, disrupted bacterial membranes, and interlinked bacteria, and cells stuck to each other (Fig. 2b). The current study demonstrated that charged CDs (−47.6 mV) interacted with the negatively charged (-4.95 mV) cell wall of E. coli, leading to the internalization of CDs and cell death. Once they enter the bacterial cell, CDs interact with organelles, resulting in cytoplasmic material leakage, ROS generation, and ultimately, bacterial death. The SEM images revealed the lysis of E. coli cells, which was similar to the observations reported by Ye et al. (2020). SEM findings support the bactericidal activity of CDs against E. coli, which is consistent with the antibacterial test results of our previous study (Ghorbani et al., 2022). The ability of CDs to perform electrostatic interactions with bacterial cells depends on the surface charge of both CDs and bacteria. Negative and positively charged CDs cause bacterial death, whereas uncharged CDs do not affect cell growth (Bing et al., 2016).

The exposure to CDs reduced the length of E. coli cells, indicating the inhibition of bacterial growth. According to Song et al. (2018), CDs can penetrate into cell walls and membranes of E. coli without causing cell damage, resulting in only slight shrinkage of cells. The researchers outlined three mechanisms by which CDs impede bacterial growth: a) electrostatic attraction causing CDs to adsorb onto the bacterial surface, b) nanoscale size enabling CDs to diffuse into bacterial cells, and c) disruption of the double-helix structure of DNA while maintaining cell morphology. Some studies have confirmed that CDs can disrupt E. coli cell membranes and cause leakage of cytoplasmic contents (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022; Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, Roy, et al., 2022; Priyadarshi et al., 2021).

The microbial count and chemical characteristics (i.e., pH, TBARS, and TVB-N) of minced beef that was stored in the refrigerator for 9 days and packaged with BC, BC-CDs16.50, and BC-CDs22.50 are presented in Table 1 .

In this study, the effectiveness of BC-CDs in controlling the TMC and TPC during refrigerated storage was assessed. As shown in Table 1, the TMC and TPC levels in both the control and BC samples increased to some extent throughout the storage period. However, the rate of increase in the BC samples was slower than that of the control samples. This outcome could potentially be attributed to the water absorption property of BC membranes, which may have inhibited bacterial growth in meat. These changes align with the results reported by Yuan et al. (2022), on the use of BC in refrigerated meat. By the third day of storage, the TMC levels in the BC-CDs samples had decreased, indicating the rapid release of CDs into the meat during the initial days. Throughout the storage period, as the TMC and TPC levels continued to increase, samples with BC-CDs22.50 exhibited lower rates of increase than samples with BC-CDs16.50, indicating that the antibacterial activity of BC-CDs was dependent on the CDs content. The initial TMC was 5.2 ± 0.0 log10 CFU/g and given that the maximum acceptable threshold for TMC in meat is 7.0 log10 CFU/g (Shafipour Yordshahi et al., 2020), both the control and samples with BC had completely deteriorated by the sixth day.

Samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 had TMC levels of 6.3 ± 0.2 and 5.8 ± 0.1 log10 CFU/g, respectively. By the ninth day, 3 and 2.3 log10 CFU/g reduction in samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50, were reported, respectively, compared to control samples. TPC levels increased in all samples with storage time. If the TPC of raw minced beef exceeds 6.7 log10 CFU/g (Trabelsi et al., 2019), it is unsuitable for consumption. The initial TPC was 4.0 ± 0.1 log CFU/g, and by the end of the storage, the counts in control and samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 reached 8.4 ± 0.0, 6.7 ± 0.0, and 6.5 ± 0.1 log10 CFU/g, respectively, indicating a significant distinction between the control group and the samples containing BC-CDs. The addition of CDs to BC membranes significantly reduced the bacterial growth rate (P < 0.05) compared to of that the control and samples with BC.

The pH values of both the control and samples with BC increased rapidly during the storage (Table 1). The increase in pH can be attributed to the decomposition of proteins into alkaline compounds and volatile bases such as amines and trimethylamines by microorganisms or enzymes (Alirezalu et al., 2021). A decrease in pH was observed in samples with BC-CDs16.50 (pH 5.3) and BC-CDs22.50 (pH 5.2) on the 3rd day. This is associated with the increased release of CDs into the meat and CDs inhibitory effects on bacterial growth, or the acidic nature of the CDs.

By the 3th and 9th days, no significant difference was observed in the pH of the BC and control samples. Generally, the pH values increased simultaneously with an increase in the total volatile bases and microbial load. Samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 had pH values of 5.7 and 5.6, respectively, whereas the control and samples with BC were spoiled. However, on the last day of storage, the pH values of the samples with BC-CDs were significantly (P < 0.05) lower than control and BC samples. The pH is an important factor that can influence the antimicrobial activity of each antibacterial substance in meat (Alirezalu et al., 2021). BC-CDs, particularly BC-CDs22.50, inhibited bacterial growth and reduced the accumulation of bacterial metabolites. In a previous study on the use of CDs in edible films, it was found that a gelatin-chitosan film containing CDs was highly effective in protecting fish meat. Throughout the storage period, CDs helped maintain low total bacterial counts and pH values (Fu et al., 2022).

The TBARS value serves as an indicator of the amount of malondialdehyde produced by the breakdown of unsaturated fatty acids via oxidation reactions in meat (Alirezalu et al., 2021). The initial TBARS values for all samples (Table 1) were 0.3 ± 0.1 mg MDA/kg. While the value remained constant in all samples except the control for the first 3 days, the control sample exhibited the highest increase in TBARS value over the storage period. For the BC membranes, this may be related to the limited imposition of oxygen diffusion to the meat sample's surface which prevent oxidation (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023). However, for BC-CDs membranes, the ability of CDs to inhibit free radicals can decrease the degree of oxidation, although the antioxidant attributes of CDs are mainly due to the electron transfer reaction mechanism rather than the scavenging performance (Ghorbani et al., 2022). Antioxidants can convert free radicals into stable products by donating hydrogen atoms, thereby preventing their spread. Several studies have indicated that CDs possess abundant hydroxyl functional groups on their surface, serving as effective hydrogen donors and exhibiting exceptional capabilities for scavenging hydroxyl and superoxide anions (Zhao, Zhang, & Wang, 2022).

Although TBARS values increased in all samples from the 3rd day to the end of storage, samples containing BC-CDs membranes showed significantly lower (P < 0.05) values than the control sample. Samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 membranes exhibited TBARS values of 0.6 ± 0.1 and 0.7 ± 0.1, respectively, on the 9th day. Moreover, the TBARS values for the samples with BC-CDs membranes remained below the critical point of 2 mg MDA/kg, which is the threshold for meat oxidation detection by consumers (Amjadi et al., 2020). These results are consistent with those of Fu et al. (2022), who observed that the TBA values for chitosan–gelatin-CDs films increased at a slower rate compared to films without CDs.

Changes in pH and TVB-N levels during meat storage are influenced by the accumulation of alkaline compounds generated by bacterial enzymatic activities. Generally, a TVB-N content of 15 mg/100 g in meat indicates spoilage (Zeng et al., 2023). The initial TVB-N value was 2 mg/100 g in all samples (Table 1), which represents the freshness of minced meat. However, TVB-N values of the control samples increased during storage. On the 3rd day, there was a decline in the TVB-N value in samples with BC-CDs, which was proportional to the reduction in the growth of microorganisms, which may be due to the fast release of CDs from the BC membrane into the meat during the first days of storage. This is linked to the antimicrobial effects of BC. Notably, microbial proliferation rates and TVB-N values exhibited similar patterns during storage. By the 9th day, both control and samples with BC showed microbial contamination. However, the TVB-N value of samples with BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 were 13.3 ± 0.9 and 9.1 ± 0.9 mg/100 g, respectively, indicating a decrease in biochemical reactions and the production of volatile compounds, especially in samples with BC-CDs22.50. Furthermore, changes in TVB-N values were positively correlated with pH variations among all samples. Other studies have also supported a connection between TVB-N and microbial counts in meat (Bekhit et al., 2021). Zhao, Zhang, Wang, et al. (2022) reported that the TVB-N content of fried meatballs in oils containing CDs was low because of the antibacterial properties of CDs. Additionally, a significant decrease in TVB-N value was observed in trout fillets coated with Persian gum/gelatin films containing CDs during 8-day storage at 4 °C (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023).

The appearance of meat is crucial because it affects consumer acceptance and color plays a significant role in this regard. Table 2 illustrates the changes in the color (L*, a*, and b*) of packaged minced meat. The discoloration of minced meat during storage is related to the conversion of myoglobin/oxymyoglobin (red pigment in raw meat) to metmyoglobin (brown pigment) by oxidation (Min et al., 2023). Furthermore, the oxidation of lipids can affect the color and flavor of meat owing to the resulting products of lipid oxidation, which then triggers the oxidation of myoglobin (X. Wang et al., 2021).

The L* value indicates the brightness of the meat, and a higher value indicates a better gloss. During storage, the L* values of all samples declined, with BC-CDs22.50 exhibiting the fastest rate of decrease, and samples with BC showed the slowest rate. The decreasing in the L* value of all samples was due to oxidation, which changed the meat color from red to brown (Sayadi et al., 2022). These findings are consistent with those of Yuan et al. (2022), who found that BC membranes had a lower declining trend in L* values than the control samples (Yuan et al., 2022).

The meat with BC-CDs22.50 had the lowest L* values among all the samples on the 9th day (P < 0.05), suggesting that increasing the concentration of CDs resulted in a decrease in the L* value of the samples due to the yellow–brown color of the CDs. On the other hand, Sul et al. reported that chitosan/gelatin/CDs film caused decreased L* values of minced meat due to bleaching of myoglobin and blackening of the minced meat (Sul et al., 2023).

Typically, the a* value is used to indicate changes in the color quality of red minced meat during storage. Previous studies have reported that oxidation causes a reduction in a* value of meat (Alirezalu et al., 2021). The decrease in a* values may be attributed to the oxidation of oxymyoglobin to metmyoglobin (Amjadi et al., 2020). The meat with BC membranes exhibited the highest a* values on the 3rd and 6th days, potentially due to their ability to trap oxygen, leading to the oxidation of iron in myoglobin heme and the creation of a red pigment that maintained the redness of the meat. Samples with BC-CDs22.50 had the highest a* value (11.5 ± 4.8) on day 9 (P < 0.05), which was ascribed to the antioxidant activity of CDs. In addition, discoloration can occur due to complex formation between myoglobin and compounds such as H2O2 and H2S, which are produced by microorganisms (Lin et al., 2022). On the other hand, the meat with BC membranes exhibited the highest a* values on the 3rd and 6th days, potentially due to their ability to trap oxygen, leading to the oxidation of iron in myoglobin and the creation of oxymyoglobin that maintained the redness of the meat.

The b* value represents yellowness, and the low levels observed in the all samples may be linked to browning reactions that occur in minced meat (Alirezalu et al., 2021). The decrease in oxymyoglobin and increase in the metmyoglobin content of meat are responsible for the decline in the b* value (Sayadi et al., 2022). Samples with BC-CDs22.50 showed the lowest b* values. In summary, the addition of CDs resulted in a decrease in the L* and b* values and an increase in the a* value of the samples because of the yellow–brown color of the CDs.

The color of meat with BC-CDs changed more than that of the other samples on day 9 (Table 3
), possibly because of the release of CDs in the meat and the darkening in the color of the meat. The control samples had the highest scores (P < 0.05) for color compared with the other samples. The presence of off-odors in the control samples may be attributed to protein degradation during storage. The odor and overall acceptability ratings declined for all samples during storage, but the samples with BC-CDs had significantly higher scores than the control and samples with only BC (P < 0.05) because of the smoked aroma of the CDs. No significant differences in odor were observed between meat with BC and control samples on days 6 and 9 (P > 0.05). Similar sensory results have been reported when comparing unwrapped and BC-wrapped beef (Gedarawatte et al., 2020).

The PCA method was used to conduct multivariate analysis of the studied parameters. The first two components accounted for 87.7% of the data variation. The vector angle of pH, TBA, TMC, TVB-N, and TPC parameters was <90°; indicating a positive correlation, and placing them in one group. Conversely, qualitative parameters including color, odor, and acceptability formed another group and showed a direct relationship with each other. These two parameter groups exhibited opposite characteristics, leading to a negative relationship between them (Fig. 3 ). However, Fig. 3B displays a favorable distribution of treatments at different times. PC1 effectively separated the studied times, making it the “time component.” Meanwhile, PC2 distinguished the treatments from each other. The control samples and certain treatments (BC, BC-CDs16.50, and BC-CDs22.50) exhibited similar distributions to qualitative traits on the initial day (0 day) and to some extent on the third day. This indicates that these treatments had high values for this group of parameters. Such straightforward and clear interpretations of the PCA biplot highlight the desirable features of this multivariate statistical method, as observed in some food health studies (Ben Hsouna et al., 2022).

CDs possess promising antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, which make them potentially useful for food packaging applications. In this study, BC membranes were used to develop antimicrobial nanopapers containing two different concentrations of CDs obtained from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae. BC-CDs16.50 and BC-CDs22.50 exhibited no significant toxicity against the HEK and HCT-116 cell lines. Moreover, BC-CDs22.50 displayed the highest release rate in water and 10% ethanol solutions. The BC-CDs membrane was shown to enhance the safety and extend the shelf life of minced beef during 9-day storage at 4 °C. The meat samples treated with BC-CDs membranes exhibited lower TMC and TPC. The CDs released from BC into the meat prevented lipid and protein oxidation by scavenging free radicals, resulting in a decreased TVB-N content and lower TBARS values. Taking into account all shelf life factors, the use of BC-CDs membranes led to a prolongation of the shelf life of minced meat by more than 3 days. The release of CDs from the membrane into the meat matrix imparted a smoky aroma to the samples, thereby positively influencing their overall acceptance. Moreover, the BC-CDs membrane also exhibited antimicrobial activity against E. coli, with BC-CDs22.50 exhibiting higher activity than BC-CDs16.50. In conclusion, BC-CDs membranes can be used in active packaging applications to prevent the quality deterioration of meat products.

Mahdi Ghorbani: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Hossein Tajik: Supervision, Visualization. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Arash Alizadeh: Investigation.
